content.body:
## Summary
Evolve ART from a local single-user tool into a hosted multi-user SaaS. This is the foundational issue that unlocks the dataset, analytics, and fine-tuning roadmap. Requires hosted database, user authentication, and per-tenant data isolation.

## Goals
- Hosted database (PostgreSQL on RDS, Supabase, or similar) replacing local SQLite
- User authentication (email/password + GitHub OAuth)
- Row-level security and IAM-based access control so users only see their own data
- Encrypted connections enforced
- Migration path from existing local SQLite DBs
- Multi-user session management in the TUI/web layer

## Why this matters
Everything else on the SaaS roadmap — dataset collection, analytics, fine-tuning — depends on a centralized DB with proper tenant isolation. This is the prerequisite.
content.title: SaaS multi-user architecture: auth, tenant isolation, hosted DB

items.title: SaaS multi-user architecture: auth, tenant isolation, hosted DB

Move from local SQLite to a hosted database (e.g. PostgreSQL on RDS or Supabase) with IAM-based access control, row-level security, and encrypted connections — so the app can support multiple users or remote deployments without exposing credentials.

## Goals
- Evaluate hosting options (RDS, Supabase, PlanetScale, etc.)
- Implement IAM-based authentication (no hardcoded DB credentials)
- Add row-level security policies for multi-user isolation
- Encrypted connections (TLS) enforced
- Migration path from existing local SQLite DBs

content.body:
## Summary
Log every resume, job description, ATS score, tailoring result, and revision across users to build a proprietary dataset. This dataset is the foundation for fine-tuning a domain-specific tailoring model and building analytics on what actually works.

## Goals
- Log all tailoring events: input resume, job description, ATS score before/after, AI output, user revisions
- Log skill match and gap data per job application
- Store structured enough to use as fine-tuning training data (input/output pairs)
- Analytics layer: aggregated views on which skills are most in-demand, which tailoring strategies improve ATS scores
- Consent and data privacy controls — users must opt in to data collection
- GDPR/CCPA compliant data handling

## Why this matters
At scale this becomes a proprietary dataset no one else has — real signal on what resume tailoring approaches actually improve outcomes. Enables fine-tuning ART's own tailoring model instead of relying purely on general-purpose LLMs, and creates a meaningful moat.

## Dependencies
- Requires #14 (SaaS multi-user architecture) to be in place first
content.title: Usage dataset and analytics layer for model training

items.title: Usage dataset and analytics layer for model training

content.body:
## Summary
Chat history currently resets between sessions. Persist conversation history to the database so returning to a job restores the full prior chat context, and the assistant can reference what was discussed in earlier sessions.

## Goals
- Save all chat turns (user + assistant) to the DB per job
- Restore history when a job is re-selected after app restart
- Ensure DB write failures don't affect the chat experience
- Auto-migrate existing DBs without breaking them

## Completed
- `ChatMessage` DB model added with `job_id`, `role`, `content`, and `created_at` fields
- `save_chat_message()` in `tui/services.py` persists each user and assistant turn; never raises on failure
- `load_chat_history()` retrieves the last N messages for a job, oldest-first, returning `[]` on error
- Auto-prune via `_prune_chat_messages()` — keeps at most 100 messages per job to prevent unbounded growth
- History injected into the LangGraph agent context on job selection (`agents/chat.py`, limit=20)
- TUI restores rendered history into the chat panel when switching jobs (`tui/app.py`)

## Future Improvements
- **Context window management** — the 20-message load limit is hardcoded; older turns are silently dropped. Needs summarization or a configurable sliding window (see #6)
- **Configurable limits** — `_MAX_CHAT_MESSAGES_PER_JOB` (100) and the load limit (20) are magic numbers; expose via env var
- **Timestamp visibility** — stored `created_at` timestamps are never shown in the UI; surfacing them would improve readability of long conversations
- **Cross-session continuity for the LLM** — restored history is injected as raw messages but the LLM may not fully leverage old context without a summarization pass
content.title: Persistent chat memory across sessions

items.title: Persistent chat memory across sessions

content.body:
## Summary

Four correctness and quality improvements to the chat agent's context management system. Changes live in `agents/chat.py`.

---

## Fix 1: Cumulative summary overwrite (correctness bug)

When `_maybe_compress_history()` fires a second time (history regrows to 30 after a prior compression), it only summarizes the 22 newest messages — the prior summary is never included. The old summary gets silently overwritten and the earliest context is permanently lost.

**Fix:** Before building the compression prompt, prepend the existing `_job_summaries` entry (if any) so the LLM is summarizing the prior summary + new messages together, not just the new messages. This rolls context forward correctly across multiple compression cycles.

---

## Fix 2: Env var configurability (original goal, never implemented)

`_COMPRESS_AT = 30`, `_COMPRESS_KEEP = 8`, and `budget_tokens = 6000` are all hardcoded module-level constants. Expose them via env vars with the existing values as defaults:

| Env var | Default | Controls |
|---|---|---|
| `ART_COMPRESS_AT` | `30` | Message count that triggers summarization |
| `ART_COMPRESS_KEEP` | `8` | Messages retained after compression |
| `ART_CONTEXT_BUDGET` | `6000` | Token budget for `_build_context_window` |

Read at module load time so tests can override with `monkeypatch.setenv`.

---

## Fix 3: 300-char truncation in compression prompt drops meaningful context

`_maybe_compress_history` truncates each message to 300 characters (`content[:300]`) before sending to the LLM summarizer. For long assistant responses (full tailored resume content, skill analysis output), this silently drops the majority of the message before the summarizer sees it.

**Fix:** Replace the fixed 300-char cap with a per-message token budget. Allocate the summarization prompt's total token budget (e.g. 4000 tokens, approximated as `len // 4`) across the messages to summarize, truncating proportionally rather than by a fixed character limit.

---

## Fix 4: System prompt and summary tokens not counted against context budget

`_build_context_window` uses `len // 4` to budget the history messages it returns, but the system prompt and injected summary block (lines 1255–1258 in `agents/chat.py`) are never counted against that budget. On a long conversation with a large prior summary, the actual LLM call can exceed the model's context window.

**Fix:** Accept an optional `reserved_tokens` parameter in `_build_context_window` and subtract it from `budget_tokens` before iterating. At the call site, pass an estimate of the system prompt + summary size so the window is sized correctly end-to-end.

---

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Cumulative compression rolls prior summary forward (Fix 1)
- [ ] `ART_COMPRESS_AT`, `ART_COMPRESS_KEEP`, `ART_CONTEXT_BUDGET` env vars control the constants (Fix 2)
- [ ] Compression prompt truncates proportionally, not at 300 chars (Fix 3)
- [ ] `_build_context_window` accepts and applies `reserved_tokens` (Fix 4)
- [ ] Tests cover each fix (env var override, cumulative summary, budget accounting)
- [ ] `python run_tests.py` passes
- [ ] Commit as: `fix(chat): context window correctness — cumulative summary, env vars, budget accounting`

## Dependencies
None
content.title: Context window management for long chat sessions

items.title: Context window management for long chat sessions

content.body:
## Summary

Three TUI bug fixes in `tui/app.py` and `agents/chat.py`.

---

## Bug 1: Delete button does not immediately delete a job

Clicking the × button in the sidebar job list should call `_delete_job()` and refresh the sidebar immediately. Trace the `on_button_pressed` handler to find where the delete action is handled. Make sure `services.delete_job(job_uuid)` is called and the sidebar reloads without any additional user step required.

## Bug 2: Pasting into the chat input only captures the first line

The `#chat-input` widget is a single-line Textual `Input`, which strips newlines on paste. Replace it with a `TextArea` widget so multi-line pastes are preserved as a single block of text. The send behavior should not change: pressing Enter or clicking Send submits the full content as one chat message. Update any references to the widget type (`query_one`, CSS selectors, etc.) and verify the submit path in `_handle_chat_input()` still works.

## Bug 3: The TUI interface disappears when tailoring runs

The tailoring workflow runs in a background thread via `_run_chat()` or a worker. An unhandled exception in that thread likely causes the app to exit instead of showing an error. Wrap the background tailoring call in a try/except block. On failure, post the error message back to the chat panel as a system message (css class `.system-msg`) so the user sees what went wrong without the TUI crashing. Check `agents/chat.py _tailor_active_job()` and the call site in `tui/app.py`.

---

## Acceptance Criteria

- At least one test per bug fix in `tests/test_tui.py`
- `python run_tests.py` passes
- Commit as: `fix(tui): delete button auto-confirm, multi-line paste, tailoring crash handling`
content.title: fix(tui): delete button auto-confirm, multi-line paste, tailoring crash handling

items.title: fix(tui): delete button auto-confirm, multi-line paste, tailoring crash handling

content.body:
## Summary

Change the tailored resume export format from Markdown to PDF. Changes live in `agents/tailor.py` and `agents/chat.py`.

---

## Implementation

Currently `agents/tailor.py` writes the tailored content to:
```
exports/tailored_resume_{safe_title}_{timestamp}.md
```

Change this to produce a PDF:
```
exports/tailored_resume_{safe_title}_{timestamp}.pdf
```

### Steps

1. Keep the LLM-generated tailored content as the source of truth (Markdown text).
2. Convert that Markdown to styled HTML (use the `markdown` package or `mistune`).
3. Convert the HTML to PDF using `weasyprint` (preferred for CSS support) or `pdfkit`. Add whichever library is chosen to `requirements.txt` / `pyproject.toml`.
4. Apply a clean, professional CSS stylesheet that mirrors typical resume structure:
   - Clear section headings, consistent font, 1-inch margins.
   - Match the section order to whatever section order the original ingested resume used.
   - The original resume path is accessible via `services.get_resume_path(user_id)`. Use it to infer section ordering if possible; otherwise use a sensible default order (Summary → Experience → Projects → Skills → Education).
5. Update `UserJobResult.export_path` to the `.pdf` path.
6. Update the chat confirmation message in `agents/chat.py _export_active_job()` to say "PDF" instead of "Markdown file".

Do not change the DB schema — `UserJobResult.export_path` is already a plain string column. Keep the `.md` intermediate file only if it simplifies the pipeline; do not expose it to the user.

---

## Acceptance Criteria

- Test in `tests/test_prd04.py` verifies the export file has a `.pdf` extension
- `python run_tests.py` passes
- Commit as: `feat(tailor): export tailored resume as PDF`
content.title: feat(tailor): export tailored resume as PDF

items.title: feat(tailor): export tailored resume as PDF

content.body:
## Summary

Two related improvements to the post-tailoring chat experience. Changes live in `agents/tailor.py`, `agents/chat.py`, and `tui/services.py`.

---

## Part A: Add a "what was changed" section to the tailoring response

In `agents/tailor.py` or `agents/chat.py _tailor_active_job()`, after the LLM produces the tailored content and the summary of what was emphasized or inferred, append a new bullet-point section explicitly listing what was **added or altered**. Examples:
- "Added Python to Skills section"
- "Added 'reduced API latency by 40%' bullet to Software Engineer at Acme"
- "Reworded project description for Capstone to emphasize ML pipeline work"

Derive this list by diffing the original resume content against the tailored content, or by prompting the LLM as part of the tailoring step to explicitly enumerate its changes. Label the section clearly, e.g.:
```
Changes made:
- ...
```

---

## Part B: "Add missing skill" chat command

After tailoring, the chat response lists missing skills. The user should be able to follow up with a message like:
- `"add missing skill Python"`
- `"add Docker to my DevOps Engineer experience"`
- `"add Kubernetes to the cloud infrastructure project"`

In `agents/chat.py`, add a fast-path route for this intent (follow existing routing patterns). The handler should:

1. Parse the skill name from the message.
2. Optionally parse a target: an experience title or project name if the user specifies one.
3. Call a new service function `add_skill_to_profile(user_id, skill_name, target=None)` in `tui/services.py`:
   - If no target: insert into the user's Skills table with `source="user-added"`.
   - If a target experience or project is named: associate the skill with that entry (look up the experience/project by title substring match and link via the appropriate join table or skill evidence record).
4. Return a confirmation string, e.g.:
   - `"Added 'Python' to your Skills section."`
   - `"Added 'Docker' to DevOps Engineer at Acme Corp."`

The function must not raise — catch any DB errors and return a plain error string instead. Follow the contract in `agents/CLAUDE.md`: tool functions return plain-English strings.

---

## Acceptance Criteria

- Routing tests in `tests/test_chat.py`
- Service test in `tests/test_services.py`
- `python run_tests.py` passes
- Commit as: `feat(tailor): change-summary output and add-missing-skill command`
content.title: feat(tailor): change-summary output and add-missing-skill command

items.title: feat(tailor): change-summary output and add-missing-skill command

content.body:
## Summary

Three improvements to the Profile screen in the ART TUI. UI changes live in `tui/screens/profile.py`. Schema/service changes in `database/` and `tui/services.py`.

---

## Improvement 1: Add an exit/back button

`ProfileScreen` currently dismisses only on Esc. Add a `Button` (label "Back" or "Close") that calls `self.dismiss()` when pressed. Place it at the bottom of the modal or top-right corner. Do not remove the Esc key binding — keep both paths working.

---

## Improvement 2: Add contact info fields

Add three new editable `Input` fields to `ProfileScreen`:
- Phone number (label "Phone")
- Email address (label "Email")
- Location formatted as "City, ST" — city name + two-letter state abbreviation (label "Location")

These must be persisted. Add `Optional` columns to the relevant SQLModel model (likely `UserProfile` or the `User` model in `database/`). Use `Optional[str] = None` defaults so existing local DBs load without migration. Update:
- `tui/services.py get_profile_data()` to return the three new fields
- `tui/services.py update_profile()` (or a new `update_profile_contact()` method) to accept and save the three new fields

Keep schema changes backward-compatible per the architecture invariants in `CLAUDE.md`.

---

## Improvement 3: Add an "Update" save button

Currently edits to profile fields are lost on close. Add an "Update" `Button` that, when clicked:
1. Reads the current values of all editable fields (name, github_username, linkedin_url, phone, email, location).
2. Calls the appropriate service method to persist them.
3. Shows a brief confirmation (e.g., update a `Static` label to "Saved." for 2 seconds, then clear it, or use `app.notify()`).

Do not auto-save on every keystroke — only save on button click.

---

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests in `tests/test_tui.py` and `tests/test_services.py`
- `python run_tests.py` passes
- Commit as: `feat(profile): exit button, contact info fields, update button`
content.title: feat(profile): exit button, contact info fields, update button

items.title: feat(profile): exit button, contact info fields, update button

content.body:
## Summary

Four data-quality fixes for the Skills system. Changes span `tui/services.py`, `tui/app.py` (`_load_skills_table`), and the ingestion pipeline.

---

## Fix 1: Source field display labels

In `tui/services.py get_skills()` (or in `tui/app.py _load_skills_table()` where skills are rendered into the Tree), fix the source field:
- If the source indicates GitHub: display `"GitHub: {repo_name}"` where `repo_name` is the specific repository name (e.g., `"GitHub: my-project"`). Extract this from however the source string or evidence record stores the repo reference.
- If the source indicates a resume file: display just `"resume"`, stripping the full file path or filename.

Apply these transformations before display; do not modify stored DB values.

---

## Fix 2: Consolidate duplicate skill entries in the Skills display

In `tui/app.py _load_skills_table()`, before populating the Tree, group skills by normalized name (lowercase, stripped). For each unique skill name, merge all entries into one display row:
- Proficiency: use the value from the highest-confidence entry.
- Confidence: use the maximum confidence across entries.
- Sources: join all unique source labels (after Fix 1 formatting) with `", "`. Example: `"resume, GitHub: my-project"`

This is display-only — do not alter DB records. The Tree should show one node per unique skill name.

---

## Fix 3: Prevent double-counting skills from multiple resumes

In `tui/services.py ingest_resume_file()` or wherever resume skills are written to the DB, before inserting a skill check if a skill with the same normalized name already exists for this user with `source="resume"`. If it does, update the existing record's confidence rather than creating a new row. Multiple resumes mentioning the same skill should not create duplicate rows or inflate that skill's evidence count. The source field should remain `"resume"` regardless of how many resumes were ingested.

---

## Fix 4: Do not persist skills inferred from job descriptions into the user skill table

In `agents/chat.py _analyze_active_job()` or wherever job analysis writes skills, verify that inferred/missing skills detected from the job description are stored only in `UserJobResult` (or a job-scoped structure) — not in the user's global `Skill` table. If any code path currently inserts job-inferred skills into the user's `Skill` records, remove it. The user's skill graph should reflect only what is actually on their resume/GitHub/LinkedIn.

---

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests in `tests/test_services.py`
- `python run_tests.py` passes
- Commit as: `fix(skills): source labels, dedup display, resume dedup, no job-skill leakage`
content.title: fix(skills): source labels, dedup display, resume dedup, no job-skill leakage

items.title: fix(skills): source labels, dedup display, resume dedup, no job-skill leakage

content.body:
## Summary

When a user describes a skill, project, or experience during a chat session that does not already exist in the knowledge graph, the system should offer to (or automatically) create the corresponding vertex/edge in the graph. These artifacts must persist independently of the chat — deleting the chat history for a job must not remove any knowledge graph entries that were created from it.

## Motivation

The chat panel is a natural place for users to surface knowledge about themselves that hasn't been ingested yet (e.g. "I built a distributed cache at my last job using Redis"). Today that context lives only in the chat transcript and is lost when the chat is deleted. Capturing it as a structured artifact makes it available for future tailoring runs.

## Scope

### Detection
- After each user message (or on explicit command), scan the user's message for mentions of skills, projects, or experiences not currently in the knowledge graph.
- Use the LLM to extract candidate artifacts (skill name + category, or project title + description snippet, or experience entry).

### Persistence
- On confirmation (explicit command like `/save` or a button in the chat panel), write the artifact to the appropriate DB table (`Skill` + `UserSkill`, `Project`, or `Experience`) via `tui/services.py`.
- Store a back-reference on the artifact so it can be traced to its origin chat session (optional metadata field, not a hard FK — to avoid cascade-delete coupling).
- Deleting a job's chat history must NOT delete these rows. The artifacts belong to the user profile, not to the job.

### UI
- Surface extracted candidates inline in the chat panel (e.g. a collapsible suggestion block).
- Provide a way to accept or dismiss each candidate without leaving the chat.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] A new `services.py` function creates a skill, project, or experience row from structured input and returns a plain-English result string.
- [ ] The LLM extraction step is isolated and unit-testable with a fixture response.
- [ ] Deleting a job (and its chat) does not remove any knowledge graph rows created from that chat.
- [ ] At least one test covers the services-layer creation path.
- [ ] At least one test covers the non-deletion invariant (create artifact, delete job, artifact still exists).

## Out of scope

- Automatic (no-confirmation) writes to the knowledge graph.
- Duplicate-detection beyond a simple name/category equality check.
- Editing or merging existing artifacts from the chat panel.
content.title: feat: extract knowledge artifacts from chat into the knowledge graph

items.title: feat: extract knowledge artifacts from chat into the knowledge graph

content.body:
## Summary

The resume exporter currently uses either the user's original section order (captured during ingestion via \`resume_style.section_order\`) or a fixed default. This issue adds a scoring step to the tailoring pipeline that ranks sections by how strongly their content matches the job's required skills, then passes a job-specific section order to the formatter.

A candidate applying for a research role should have Projects appear before Experience; a candidate applying for a senior engineering role should see Experience first. The order should be determined by data, not hardcoding.

## Implementation

The \`section_order\` override parameter has already been added to \`ResumeFormatterAgent.format_markdown()\` and \`format_pdf()\` as part of the style-capture implementation — this issue wires it up from the tailoring pipeline.

### Steps

1. **Add \`_score_section_relevance\`** in \`agents/chat.py\` or a shared utility:
   - Input: section name, \`tailored_content\` dict, \`matched_skills\` dict
   - Score = fraction of the section's content tokens that overlap with matched skill names
   - Return a float per section; higher = more relevant

2. **Compute ranked order in \`_tailor_active_job()\`** after the pipeline runs:
   - Score each reorderable section: \`experience\`, \`projects\`, \`skills\`
   - Prepend fixed sections (\`education\`) that should not move
   - Store the ranked list in the state or as an instance variable so \`_export_active_job()\` can read it

3. **Pass the ranked order in \`_export_active_job()\`**:
   ```python
   pdf_bytes = formatter.format_pdf(
       latest.tailored_resume_content,
       section_order=self._last_section_order,
   )
   ```

4. **Fall back gracefully**: if no ranked order is available (e.g. export called without tailor), use \`resume_style.section_order\` from the DB (already the formatter default).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] \`_score_section_relevance\` function exists and is unit-testable with a fixture response
- [ ] \`_tailor_active_job()\` stores a ranked section list after each tailoring run
- [ ] \`_export_active_job()\` passes it to \`formatter.format_pdf(section_order=...)\`
- [ ] At least one test in \`tests/test_prd04.py\` verifies that different job skill profiles produce different section orderings
- [ ] \`python run_tests.py\` passes
- [ ] Commit as: \`feat(tailor): dynamic section reordering by job relevance\`

## Dependencies

Depends on the style-capture implementation (commit \`d8d3842\`) which added the \`section_order\` parameter to the formatter.
content.title: feat(tailor): dynamic section reordering by job relevance

items.title: feat(tailor): dynamic section reordering by job relevance

content.body:
## Summary
Sub-issue of #5 (Persistent chat memory across sessions).

Stored `created_at` timestamps on `ChatMessage` are never surfaced in the UI. Show a subtle day-separator header (e.g. `── May 14 ──`) between messages from different calendar days so users can orient themselves in long conversations.

## Goals
- `load_chat_history()` returns `created_at` (ISO string) alongside `role`/`content`
- Both TUI rendering sites (`on_mount` and job-select) insert a `.day-separator` Static widget when the calendar day changes between messages
- `.day-separator` styled in `app.tcss` (centered, muted color)

## Acceptance criteria
- Day separator appears between messages from different calendar days
- Single-day conversations show no separator
- `load_chat_history` test asserts `created_at` is present in returned dicts
- TUI test asserts separator widget is mounted when messages span two days

## Dependencies
Blocked by: None
content.title: Chat TUI: show day-separator timestamps in chat panel

items.title: Chat TUI: show day-separator timestamps in chat panel

content.body:
## Summary
Sub-issue of #5 (Persistent chat memory across sessions).

`_job_summaries` in `ChatAgent` is in-memory only. When `_maybe_compress_history()` generates a summary during a session, it is lost on restart. On reload, the LLM gets raw truncated history with no summary of the older conversation.

## Goals
- Persist the per-job summary to `JobDescription.chat_summary` whenever `_maybe_compress_history()` runs
- Load the persisted summary back into `_job_summaries` in `set_active_job()` so the LLM has cross-session context continuity
- DB migration: `ALTER TABLE jobdescription ADD COLUMN chat_summary TEXT`

## Changes
- `database/models.py` — add `chat_summary: Optional[str] = None` to `JobDescription`
- `database/db.py` — add migration statement
- `tui/services.py` — add `save_chat_summary(job_id, summary)` and `load_chat_summary(job_id)`
- `agents/chat.py` — `_maybe_compress_history()` calls `save_chat_summary`; `set_active_job()` calls `load_chat_summary`

## Acceptance criteria
- `save_chat_summary` / `load_chat_summary` round-trip in tests
- After restart, `set_active_job` populates `_job_summaries` from DB
- Existing DBs auto-migrate without data loss

## Dependencies
None
content.title: Chat agent: persist and restore conversation summaries across sessions

items.title: Chat agent: persist and restore conversation summaries across sessions

content.body:
## Summary
Sub-issue of #5 (Persistent chat memory across sessions). Addresses #6 (Context window management for long chat sessions).

The LLM call uses `history[-12:]` — a hard message-count cutoff. The DB load limit is hardcoded at 20. Old context is silently dropped rather than trimmed to a token budget.

## Goals
- Replace `history[-12:]` with a token-budget approach: include as many recent messages as fit within ~6000 tokens (approx `len(content) // 4`)
- Raise the DB load limit from 20 → 50 so the budget has more messages to select from
- Older messages beyond the budget are still summarized via `_maybe_compress_history` (existing behavior)

## Changes
- `agents/chat.py` — add `_build_context_window(budget_tokens: int = 6000) -> list[dict]`
- Replace `messages.extend(self.history[-12:])` with `messages.extend(self._build_context_window())`
- `set_active_job()`: raise `load_chat_history` limit from 20 → 50

## Acceptance criteria
- `_build_context_window` never exceeds the token budget
- Messages are returned oldest-first
- A test verifies the budget is respected and ordering is correct

## Dependencies
None
content.title: Chat agent: token-budget sliding context window

items.title: Chat agent: token-budget sliding context window

content.body:
## Summary

The context window and compression pipeline (#6) makes behavioral tradeoffs — which messages to keep, when to summarize, how much history the LLM actually sees — that are not currently measurable. This issue adds a focused evaluation framework to answer: *how well does the agent retain and use past conversation context given the current window size and compression strategy?*

---

## Goals

- Measure factual recall across compression boundaries (does the agent remember what was said before a compression event?)
- Measure coherence degradation as history grows (does answer quality decline as the window fills?)
- Compare compression strategies (no compression vs. summarization vs. rolling summary from Fix 1 in #6)
- Produce a repeatable benchmark that can be re-run after any change to `_build_context_window`, `_maybe_compress_history`, or the related constants

---

## Implementation

### 1. Conversation fixture format

Define a YAML/JSON fixture format for synthetic multi-turn conversations:

```yaml
scenario: "skill recall across compression"
turns:
  - role: user
    content: "I have 4 years of experience with Kubernetes."
  - role: assistant
    content: "Got it — I'll note that."
  # ... filler turns to push past _COMPRESS_AT ...
  - role: user
    content: "How many years of Kubernetes experience do I have?"
probe:
  expected_contains: "4"
  metric: factual_recall
```

Store fixtures under `tests/memory_evals/`.

### 2. Harness (`tests/test_memory_eval.py`)

- Replay each fixture's turns through a `ChatAgent` instance with an `isolated_engine`
- After all turns, submit the probe message
- Score the response against the fixture's metric:
  - `factual_recall` — response contains `expected_contains`
  - `coherence` — LLM-as-judge scores response relevance 1–5 (optional, mark `@pytest.mark.integration`)
- Log pass/fail + the actual response to a structured report (JSON or Markdown) under `tests/memory_evals/results/`

### 3. Parameterized strategy comparison

Use `pytest.mark.parametrize` to run the same fixtures under different configurations:
- Default constants (`_COMPRESS_AT=30, _COMPRESS_KEEP=8, budget=6000`)
- Aggressive compression (`_COMPRESS_AT=10, _COMPRESS_KEEP=4`)
- No compression (disabled `_maybe_compress_history`)

Override via `monkeypatch.setenv` against the env vars added in #6 Fix 2.

### 4. Coverage targets

Write at least the following fixtures:

| Scenario | Metric |
|---|---|
| Skill mentioned in turn 2, recalled after 35 turns (past compression) | factual_recall |
| Job title stated early, referenced after window fills | factual_recall |
| User preference stated, respected in later response | factual_recall |
| Second compression: context from before first compression still available | factual_recall |
| Budget boundary: message just outside token budget is NOT recalled | negative recall |

---

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] At least 5 fixture scenarios under `tests/memory_evals/`
- [ ] `tests/test_memory_eval.py` harness runs all fast fixtures without network calls (`@pytest.mark.integration` gates LLM-judge metrics)
- [ ] Parameterized strategy comparison runs cleanly
- [ ] Results report written to `tests/memory_evals/results/` on each run
- [ ] `python run_tests.py` passes (integration tests excluded)
- [ ] Commit as: `test(chat): memory quality eval harness for context window and compression`

## Dependencies
- Blocked by #6 (env vars needed for strategy parameterization via `monkeypatch.setenv`)
content.title: test(chat): memory quality evaluation framework for context window and compression

items.title: test(chat): memory quality evaluation framework for context window and compression

content.body:
## Summary

The memory eval harness (#26) defines a \`coherence\` metric in its fixture format and documents it in the module docstring, but no fixture uses it and no test exercises it. This issue wires up the LLM-as-judge path so response quality can actually be measured, not just structural context delivery.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] At least one fixture with \`metric: coherence\` added under \`tests/memory_evals/\`
- [ ] \`test_memory_eval.py\` handles \`coherence\` fixtures: submits the probe to a **real** \`ChatAgent\` (not a capture mock), then calls a second LLM-as-judge prompt that scores the response 1–5 for relevance and factual consistency
- [ ] Coherence tests are gated with \`@pytest.mark.integration\` so they are excluded from the fast suite
- [ ] Score threshold (e.g. ≥ 3) is configurable per fixture via a \`min_score\` field
- [ ] Pass/fail + numeric score written to the results JSON alongside factual/negative recall results
- [ ] \`python run_tests.py\` (non-integration) still passes with no changes to existing tests
- [ ] \`python run_tests.py --integration -k memory_eval\` runs the coherence tests end-to-end

## Dependencies

None
content.title: test(memory-eval): implement coherence metric with LLM-as-judge scoring

items.title: test(memory-eval): implement coherence metric with LLM-as-judge scoring

Move `playwright` and `sentence-transformers` out of the base install so the Docker image is small and fast to build.

- LinkedIn PDF import still works in the base image; Playwright scraping requires the full image

content.body:
## Summary
Let users enter their AI provider and API key directly inside the TUI so Docker demo users never need to manually edit `.env`.

## Implemented ✅
This feature has already been shipped as part of the demo roadmap implementation:

- **Onboarding**: new Step 1 prompts for provider (Anthropic / OpenAI) + API key. Auto-skipped if a key is already present in the environment (for users who pre-configure via `.env` or Docker env vars).
- **Profile screen**: new LLM Provider section with provider toggle buttons + masked key field. Update button calls `services.save_llm_config()`.
- **No restart required**: `llm.py`'s `get_llm()` reads from `os.environ` at call time; `save_llm_config()` sets `os.environ` immediately on save.
- **Tests**: 6 new tests covering round-trip save, provider-only update, no-key state, and onboarding skip logic.

## Dependencies
None.
content.title: feat(tui): in-TUI API key entry — onboarding step + profile section

items.title: feat(tui): in-TUI API key entry — onboarding step + profile section
